title: Satan’s 19th-Century Bank Note
excerpt: In the early 19th century ‘filthy rags’ – or banknotes – became a common form of currency. A surge in forgery followed, accompanied by a surge in harsh prosecutions. How did we get from gold to paper?
content_html:
In the early 19th century ‘filthy rags’ – or banknotes – became a common form of currency. A surge in forgery followed, accompanied by a surge in harsh prosecutions. How did we get from gold to paper?

A view of the Bank of England, after a drawing by Thomas Hosmer Shepherd, 1816. Rijksmuseum. Public Domain.

In 1820 a satirical pamphlet called ‘Satan’s Bank Note’ appeared on the streets of London. Accompanied by a woodcut engraving of five men being executed with the devil sitting on the gallows, the pamphlet offered a biting commentary on the epidemic of forgery trials that had broken out in Britain in the years following the end of the Napoleonic Wars. The anonymous author lays the blame entirely at the door of the Bank of England and its biggest debtor, the government of the day:

Near London’s ’Change there is a house,
(To name it I’m unwilling)
Where RAGS are sold, and for each Pound
John Bull gives twenty shillings.

The anonymity of the author, and their unwillingness to explicitly name the Bank of England, stemmed, no doubt, from fear of being prosecuted for libel or sedition. Ever since William Pitt’s government had effectively suspended the gold standard in 1797, ‘poor’ John Bull had been forced to accept paper in place of gold and silver. For the first time, but by no means the last, the nation was left wondering: what exactly is money?

The recent introduction of new banknotes and coins has seen the return of this question. Does it matter what it is made of? When did money just become a convenient medium of exchange as opposed to representing intrinsic value? Or, put another way, how did we get from gold to paper? Dismissed by some as Monopoly or plastic money, the new £5 and £10 notes in particular have come in for heavy criticism. The Bank of England claims that the choice of material, polymer, along with a number of other security features, makes the new notes harder than ever to counterfeit. Staying one step ahead of the forgers is, of course, the Bank of England’s objective.   

Two hundred years ago, in 1817, the Bank of England was anything but one step ahead of the forgers. The number of forged notes and coins in circulation reached record highs amid similar debates about the nature and purpose of money. The Bank’s determination to punish those convicted of forgery led to public outcry after a series of high profile trials at the Old Bailey. An unprecedented number of people were convicted of forgery and sentenced to death. Technically a form of treason, the crime of counterfeiting was a capital one. The vast majority of those convicted were poor and in many cases women. Their crime was often not the manufacture of forged notes but ‘uttering’: being in possession of or putting into circulation forged notes. ‘The mind sickens to disgust at the hideous measures taken to sustain the blood-cemented fabric of paper currency’, the radical journalist T.J. Wooler exclaimed at the height of the controversy in October 1818.

The leading critic of the government’s monetary policy at this time was William Cobbett (1763-1835), a soldier-tory turned radical journalist. For Cobbett and the thousands of impoverished working people who supported him, paper money was not money at all. In their view, only specie – predominantly gold – was proper money. Unlike paper, precious metal had intrinsic value. This was one of the pillars of the ‘gold standard’, which brought stability. The basic tenet of the gold standard was that the number of bank notes in circulation had to be backed by a fixed quantity of gold held by the Bank of England. The ‘filthy rags’, as Cobbett termed the banknotes, were merely paper promises. As the radical Thomas Paine pointed out in the 1790s, a system of pure paper money was entirely dependent on confidence, which could vanish in a moment. Not for nothing did Paine describe paper money as ‘suspicion asleep’.

Drawing on a long line of currency theory, Paine and Cobbett argued that a currency had to be based on precious metal. This did not rule out the use of paper notes, as long as they could be exchanged for specie. It was this guarantee which gave the currency, and by extension the financial system, stability. The guarantee, which still appears on banknotes to this very day, of the promise to pay the bearer on demand the requisite sum in hard cash, was a lie after 1797. Few of us stop these days to think about what this statement actually means. Today, it is little more than a symbolic survival; but during the 18th century it had meant that paper notes could be taken to a bank and exchanged for the equivalent value in specie. They were, in other words, like cheques. This changed during the Napoleonic Wars when Pitt’s government, to stop a run on the pound when fears of a French invasion were growing, suspended convertibility in 1797.

This suspension of cash payments lasted until 1821. Poor John Bull was forced to subsist on a diet of paper money and reissued Spanish dollars seized from Spanish vessels after Spain joined with France against the British and their allies. The government was accused of hoarding gold. What went into the coffers was real money; what came out was worthless paper.

It did not take long for critics, led by Paine and later Cobbett, to read into these apparent ‘safeguards’ sinister designs by bankers and city politicians. Free of the obligation to redeem in gold debts contracted in paper, the Bank of England could in theory print as much paper money as it liked. This enabled the government to run up huge debts to finance the war against the French. While printing more money might sound like a good thing, its effect was inflation, increasing the cost of living during a period of scarcity, poor harvests and rising unemployment. As a popular journalist, Cobbett was one of the first to link the poverty of the people to the ‘money-mongering’ juggle. Week after week in his Political Register, Cobbett attacked the system of paper money and public finance. The articles were collected and published as a book in 1815, Paper against Gold. By July 1817, he claimed to have sold 150,000 copies.

The people were poor, Cobbett reasoned, because they had to pay crippling levels of indirect taxation on everyday items to pay down the exorbitant interest on the debt. Worse still, the many wars waged by the British in the 18th century had created a class of idle creditors who were able to enrich themselves by charging high levels of interest which the government was forced to to agree to finance its costly wars.

But the real scandal in Cobbett’s view was that these debts, contracted in paper money and thus hugely inflated in value, would have to be settled at some point in specie. In other words, theoretical debt would have to be paid off in real money, thus further inflating the value of the debt. It was deeply unfair, so the reasoning went, that a debt contracted in paper at, say the value of £10,000, would now be worth £100,000 because of the higher value of gold. Whose pockets would this hit? The taxpayer.

Why was the government determined to resume cash payments given the potential damage it was likely to inflict? Virtually all the experts agreed that convertibility would have to be restored to bring back sound finance and stability. This was the view taken by Lord Liverpool’s government in the 1810s, with the future Tory PM Sir Robert Peel taking charge of the legislation to resume cash payments. But how was this to be achieved without plunging the nation into financial meltdown? The amount of money in circulation would have to be reduced to bring it in line with the amount of gold held in reserve by the Bank of England. In Cobbett’s view what made this all the more unfair was that the necessary ‘belt tightening’ would fall disproportionately on the poor. Those on fixed incomes, dividends and interest payments – i.e. the creditors who had lent the money – would see no drop in the value of their investment. But ordinary people would see a fall in their wages and rising prices.

For Cobbett there was only one solution: the partial repudiation of the debt through a scaling down of its value to bring it in line with the deflated economy. This demand was regularly echoed by radicals up and down the country. Cobbett taunted the government of Lord Liverpool saying that it simply would not be able to restore convertibility without scaling down the debt. So confident was Cobbett of this that if the government achieved this he would consent to be ‘broiled alive’ on a gridiron. The gridiron became a ready-made symbol that he and his many followers adopted.

As it happens, the government did manage the feat but only, Cobbett was quick to allege, by cheating. The government empowered the Bank of England to print large quantities of small value paper notes. This was to ward off financial collapse due to money becoming scarcer as its value increased. Cobbett was proven wrong in that cash payments were eventually resumed (in May 1821), but he was correct that there was nowhere near enough gold in reserve for the Bank to redeem all of its notes in specie, just as there is not today. Cobbett refused to believe that confidence was sufficient to keep a nation’s finances afloat.

To make matters worse, this belt-tightening occurred during a period when the nation’s coinage was in serious disarray and short supply. Debasing, counterfeiting, the semi-legal use of tokens and even the use of foreign coins were all widely practised in the 1810s as a strategy of simply making do. This was the context for the dramatic rise in forgery. While forgery was not exclusive to London – Birmingham seems to have been another centre – the problem was much worse in the capital than elsewhere. This was partly due to London’s size, the sheer amount of money circulating there and the ready supply of materials and skills. But it was also because it was much easier and more profitable to forge notes in London than in the provinces. In the latter, country banks, which were independent of the Bank of England, issued their own notes and circulation was largely limited to a local and regional orbit. In this close-knit environment it was more difficult to utter forged bank notes as people were more suspicious of strangers bearing bank notes.

In London forgers faced fewer obstacles. The Bank of England seemed powerless, even though each year saw more convictions for forgery. By 1817 more than one in twenty £2 or £1 Bank of England notes were forged – and these were just the ones the Bank knew about. In 1817 the total number of forged £2 and £1 notes returned to the Bank of England was 28,648; in 2016 a mere 2,000 counterfeit £5 notes were removed from circulation. While the total number of forged notes is much higher today than it was 200 years ago, with the vast majority being counterfeit £20 notes, as a proportion of the total number of bank notes in circulation the percentage is much lower.

Sermons were preached on the sinfulness of forgery, complete with gruesome details on the psychological terror inflicted on those found guilty when sentences of death were passed. But nothing, not even the threat of death, was enough to deter the counterfeiter. The situation had become so dire by December 1817 that the Bank of England revived its defunct committee to examine plans for improvement of bank notes and prevention of forgery. Suggestions included the use of invisible ink, incredibly ornate designs drawn by artists, unique and superior kinds of paper and watermarks and the need for the most up-to-date printing technology to out-skill the forgers. The committee reported that

Great expectations are entertained by some that the science of chemistry in which such wonderful discoveries have been made of late years may be the means of improvement either in the materials of the paper or in the composition of the ink, or by supplying tests whereby to distinguish the genuine from the false note.

Eventually, the Bank adopted a combination of such features, as it continues to. As the committee conceded, the best the Bank could hope for was to stay one step ahead of the forgers:

This much is certain that the counterfeiters of the coin of the realm and the forgers of bank notes, both united … frequently in the same persons, are men of great ingenuity and of versatile talents and it is not too much to presume that their exertions will keep pace with any measures which may be adopted.

In the meantime, the Bank built up an impressive network of detectives and informers to mount one of the most organised private prosecution agencies in modern British history. Masterminded by its solicitors, Freshfields, the directors of the Bank liberally employed vast sums in prosecuting forgers. No expense was spared. Rewards were provided for information leading to convictions. Preserved in the archives of the Bank of England are confessions, plea bargains and all manner of information regarding forgery. One of the fullest accounts was furnished in 1820 by Edward Ewer, an inmate of Reading gaol. It seems that markets and fairs were at the operational centre of counterfeiting:

There is scarcely a stall keeper, fishman or horsedealer frequenting all the fairs but what deals in forged notes and base coin. They cannot live without it. They sell their articles lower than they purchase them and keep the stalls only as a blind.

Building on the platform he had constructed in Paper against Gold, Cobbett seized on the forgery cases as further evidence of the iniquities of the paper money system. Since paper money was fiction, Cobbett reasoned that there was, in fact, no difference between bank notes and forged notes; one was legal forgery, the other illegal. Capitalism and counterfeiting were different sides of the same coin: the transition to a paper-based currency and the attendant proliferation of notes, often of poor quality, created ideal conditions for counterfeiting. After all, the currency of both rested on confidence and, while forged notes might be bad money, legal notes might be issued by a bad bank (i.e. one unable to meet its obligations). The British government had even entered into the business of counterfeiting in the 1790s when it had the currency of the French revolutionaries, the assignats, forged in London and circulated in France to try and undermine the new regime.

With Cobbett in exile in the United States, having fled to avoid imprisonment for spreading sedition, it fell to T.J. Wooler to lead the public outcry. Through the pages of the Black Dwarf Wooler stoked anger against the forgery convictions, marshalling it into a determined campaign to abolish capital punishment for all crimes except murder. The necessity of such reform became very apparent during the forgery trials between September and December 1818. The trials revealed that it was difficult to distinguish between a forged and a genuine bank note, largely because of the poor quality of the latter. The arbitrary manner in which bank officials determined which notes were forged, and their refusal to divulge the criteria against which they made such determinations, outraged Wooler and the public.

Just under a third of those hanged in London in the period 1800-18 were forgers/utterers, some of them poor women driven to the crime as a means of survival. Some of the letters written to the Bank from those convicted for forgery requesting assistance attribute their deviation from the path of rectitude to the prevailing distress in the postwar years. This made the Bank’s prosecutions look like petty acts of class war. The Bank’s ruthlessness in pursuing forgers represented in microcosm the wider fears of the commercial classes. Where Cobbett and Wooler led in print, George Cruikshank and William Hone followed in visual satire by producing the most iconic rendition of the forgery crisis with his spoof counterfeit Bank Restriction Note and Barometer (1819).

The crime of counterfeiting was eventually declassified as a capital offence in 1836. Thereafter conviction rates for forgery shot up. Now that the crime was no longer a capital offence juries were more likely to return guilty verdicts. Certainly, judging by the number of trials at the Old Bailey, there had been no decline in ‘coining’ offences. The high number of offences in 1817-21 (with a peak of 78 offences in 1818), following a lull in the 1820s, were easily surpassed in the mid-1830s. There were 90 offences in 1835 alone. Yet conviction rates were on average much higher in the 1830s and 1840s than they had been in the 1810s and 20s: in 1818, 78 per cent of those on trial had been found guilty; by 1836 the figure was 99 per cent. The Bank of England had begun to turn the tables in its favour.

Two hundred years ago the Bank of England was forced to confront some of the same questions it faces today: What is money? What should it be made of? What does it represent? And what should be done regarding the attendant problems of counterfeiting? One consequence of the forgery trials was the introduction of banknotes that were more difficult to counterfeit. The new £5 and £10 notes are merely the latest in a long line of anti-counterfeiting measures. Neither are a crippling National Debt or accusations of unholy alliances between city bankers and politicians new. Cobbett would have found today’s City much as he left it in 1835.

Matthew Roberts is Senior Lecturer in Modern British History at Sheffield Hallam University.

sentences.en: How did we get from gold to paper?

blocks.html_en: In 1820 a satirical pamphlet called ‘Satan’s Bank Note’ appeared on the streets of London. Accompanied by a woodcut engraving of five men being executed with the devil sitting on the gallows, the pamphlet offered a biting commentary on the epidemic of forgery trials that had broken out in Britain in the years following the end of the Napoleonic Wars. The anonymous author lays the blame entirely at the door of the Bank of England and its biggest debtor, the government of the day:

sentences.zh: 1820年，伦敦街头出现了一本名为《撒旦的钞票》的讽刺小册子。

blocks.html_en: The anonymity of the author, and their unwillingness to explicitly name the Bank of England, stemmed, no doubt, from fear of being prosecuted for libel or sedition. Ever since William Pitt’s government had effectively suspended the gold standard in 1797, ‘poor’ John Bull had been forced to accept paper in place of gold and silver. For the first time, but by no means the last, the nation was left wondering: what exactly is money?

blocks.html_en: The recent introduction of new banknotes and coins has seen the return of this question. Does it matter what it is made of? When did money just become a convenient medium of exchange as opposed to representing intrinsic value? Or, put another way, how did we get from gold to paper? Dismissed by some as Monopoly or plastic money, the new £5 and £10 notes in particular have come in for heavy criticism. The Bank of England claims that the choice of material, polymer, along with a number of other security features, makes the new notes harder than ever to counterfeit. Staying one step ahead of the forgers is, of course, the Bank of England’s objective.

sentences.en: The recent introduction of new banknotes and coins has seen the return of this question.
sentences.zh: 最近推出的新纸币和硬币又再次引发了这个问题。

sentences.en: Does it matter what it is made of?
sentences.zh: 它是由什么制成的重要吗？

sentences.en: When did money just become a convenient medium of exchange as opposed to representing intrinsic value?
sentences.zh: 金钱什么时候才成为一种方便的交换媒介而不是代表内在价值？

sentences.en: Or, put another way, how did we get from gold to paper?
sentences.zh: 或者，换句话说，我们是如何从黄金变成纸的？

sentences.en: The Bank of England claims that the choice of material, polymer, along with a number of other security features, makes the new notes harder than ever to counterfeit.
sentences.zh: 英格兰银行声称，聚合物材料的选择以及许多其他安全功能使新纸币比以往更难伪造。

sentences.zh: 当然，英格兰银行的目标是领先伪造者一步。

blocks.html_en: Two hundred years ago, in 1817, the Bank of England was anything but one step ahead of the forgers. The number of forged notes and coins in circulation reached record highs amid similar debates about the nature and purpose of money. The Bank’s determination to punish those convicted of forgery led to public outcry after a series of high profile trials at the Old Bailey. An unprecedented number of people were convicted of forgery and sentenced to death. Technically a form of treason, the crime of counterfeiting was a capital one. The vast majority of those convicted were poor and in many cases women. Their crime was often not the manufacture of forged notes but ‘uttering’: being in possession of or putting into circulation forged notes. ‘The mind sickens to disgust at the hideous measures taken to sustain the blood-cemented fabric of paper currency’, the radical journalist T.J. Wooler exclaimed at the height of the controversy in October 1818.

sentences.en: Two hundred years ago, in 1817, the Bank of England was anything but one step ahead of the forgers.
sentences.zh: 200 年前的 1817 年，英格兰银行根本就比伪造者领先一步。

sentences.en: The number of forged notes and coins in circulation reached record highs amid similar debates about the nature and purpose of money.
sentences.zh: 在有关货币性质和用途的类似争论中，流通中的伪造纸币和硬币数量创下历史新高。

sentences.en: The Bank’s determination to punish those convicted of forgery led to public outcry after a series of high profile trials at the Old Bailey.
sentences.zh: 在老贝利进行了一系列备受瞩目的审判后，该银行决定惩罚那些被判犯有伪造罪的人，这引起了公众的强烈抗议。

sentences.en: An unprecedented number of people were convicted of forgery and sentenced to death.
sentences.zh: 被判犯有伪造罪并被判处死刑的人数空前。

sentences.en: Technically a form of treason, the crime of counterfeiting was a capital one.
sentences.zh: 从技术上讲，造假罪是一种叛国罪，是一种重罪。

sentences.en: The vast majority of those convicted were poor and in many cases women.
sentences.zh: 绝大多数被定罪的人都是穷人，其中很多是妇女。

sentences.en: Their crime was often not the manufacture of forged notes but ‘uttering’: being in possession of or putting into circulation forged notes.
sentences.zh: 他们的罪行通常不是制造伪造的纸币，而是“说出”：持有或投入流通伪造的纸币。

sentences.en: ‘The mind sickens to disgust at the hideous measures taken to sustain the blood-cemented fabric of paper currency’, the radical journalist T.J.
sentences.zh: 激进的记者 T.J.

sentences.en: Wooler exclaimed at the height of the controversy in October 1818.
sentences.zh: 1818 年 10 月，争论最激烈时，伍勒发出了这样的感叹。

blocks.html_en: The leading critic of the government’s monetary policy at this time was William Cobbett (1763-1835), a soldier-tory turned radical journalist. For Cobbett and the thousands of impoverished working people who supported him, paper money was not money at all. In their view, only specie – predominantly gold – was proper money. Unlike paper, precious metal had intrinsic value. This was one of the pillars of the ‘gold standard’, which brought stability. The basic tenet of the gold standard was that the number of bank notes in circulation had to be backed by a fixed quantity of gold held by the Bank of England. The ‘filthy rags’, as Cobbett termed the banknotes, were merely paper promises. As the radical Thomas Paine pointed out in the 1790s, a system of pure paper money was entirely dependent on confidence, which could vanish in a moment. Not for nothing did Paine describe paper money as ‘suspicion asleep’.

sentences.en: The leading critic of the government’s monetary policy at this time was William Cobbett (1763-1835), a soldier-tory turned radical journalist.
sentences.zh: 当时对政府货币政策的主要批评者是威廉·科贝特（William Cobbett，1763-1835 年），他是一名从士兵保守派转变为激进的记者。

sentences.en: For Cobbett and the thousands of impoverished working people who supported him, paper money was not money at all.
sentences.zh: 对于科贝特和成千上万支持他的贫困劳动人民来说，纸币根本不是钱。

sentences.en: In their view, only specie – predominantly gold – was proper money.
sentences.zh: 在他们看来，只有硬币——主要是黄金——才是真正的货币。

sentences.en: Unlike paper, precious metal had intrinsic value.
sentences.zh: 与纸张不同，贵金属具有内在价值。

sentences.en: This was one of the pillars of the ‘gold standard’, which brought stability.
sentences.zh: 这是带来稳定的“金本位”的支柱之一。

sentences.en: The basic tenet of the gold standard was that the number of bank notes in circulation had to be backed by a fixed quantity of gold held by the Bank of England.
sentences.zh: 金本位制的基本原则是流通中的纸币数量必须以英格兰银行持有的固定数量的黄金作为支持。

sentences.en: The ‘filthy rags’, as Cobbett termed the banknotes, were merely paper promises.
sentences.zh: 科贝特将这些钞票称为“肮脏的破布”，只不过是纸质承诺。

sentences.en: As the radical Thomas Paine pointed out in the 1790s, a system of pure paper money was entirely dependent on confidence, which could vanish in a moment.
sentences.zh: 正如激进的托马斯·潘恩在 1790 年代指出的那样，纯纸币体系完全依赖于信心，而信心可能会瞬间消失。

sentences.en: Not for nothing did Paine describe paper money as ‘suspicion asleep’.
sentences.zh: 潘恩将纸币描述为“沉睡的怀疑”并非没有道理。

blocks.html_en: Drawing on a long line of currency theory, Paine and Cobbett argued that a currency had to be based on precious metal. This did not rule out the use of paper notes, as long as they could be exchanged for specie. It was this guarantee which gave the currency, and by extension the financial system, stability. The guarantee, which still appears on banknotes to this very day, of the promise to pay the bearer on demand the requisite sum in hard cash, was a lie after 1797. Few of us stop these days to think about what this statement actually means. Today, it is little more than a symbolic survival; but during the 18th century it had meant that paper notes could be taken to a bank and exchanged for the equivalent value in specie. They were, in other words, like cheques. This changed during the Napoleonic Wars when Pitt’s government, to stop a run on the pound when fears of a French invasion were growing, suspended convertibility in 1797.

sentences.en: Drawing on a long line of currency theory, Paine and Cobbett argued that a currency had to be based on precious metal.
sentences.zh: 潘恩和科贝特借鉴了一系列的货币理论，认为货币必须以贵金属为基础。

sentences.en: This did not rule out the use of paper notes, as long as they could be exchanged for specie.
sentences.zh: 这并不排除使用纸币，只要它们可以兑换成硬币。

sentences.en: It was this guarantee which gave the currency, and by extension the financial system, stability.
sentences.zh: 正是这种保证给了货币以及金融体系的稳定性。

sentences.en: The guarantee, which still appears on banknotes to this very day, of the promise to pay the bearer on demand the requisite sum in hard cash, was a lie after 1797.
sentences.zh: 至今仍出现在纸币上的保证书承诺按要求向持票人支付所需金额的现金，但 1797 年之后却是一个谎言。

sentences.en: Few of us stop these days to think about what this statement actually means.
sentences.zh: 如今，我们很少有人停下来思考这句话的真正含义。

sentences.en: Today, it is little more than a symbolic survival; but during the 18th century it had meant that paper notes could be taken to a bank and exchanged for the equivalent value in specie.
sentences.zh: 如今，它只不过是一种象征性的生存。但在 18 世纪，这意味着可以将纸币带到银行兑换成等值的硬币。

sentences.en: They were, in other words, like cheques.
sentences.zh: 换句话说，它们就像支票。

sentences.en: This changed during the Napoleonic Wars when Pitt’s government, to stop a run on the pound when fears of a French invasion were growing, suspended convertibility in 1797.
sentences.zh: 这种情况在拿破仑战争期间发生了变化，当时皮特政府在人们对法国入侵的担忧日益加剧之际，为了阻止英镑挤兑，于 1797 年暂停了英镑兑换。

blocks.html_en: This suspension of cash payments lasted until 1821. Poor John Bull was forced to subsist on a diet of paper money and reissued Spanish dollars seized from Spanish vessels after Spain joined with France against the British and their allies. The government was accused of hoarding gold. What went into the coffers was real money; what came out was worthless paper.

sentences.en: This suspension of cash payments lasted until 1821.
sentences.zh: 这种现金支付的暂停一直持续到 1821 年。

sentences.en: Poor John Bull was forced to subsist on a diet of paper money and reissued Spanish dollars seized from Spanish vessels after Spain joined with France against the British and their allies.
sentences.zh: 西班牙与法国联手对抗英国及其盟友后，可怜的约翰·布尔被迫靠纸币和从西班牙船只上缴获的西班牙元重新发行来维持生计。

sentences.en: The government was accused of hoarding gold.
sentences.zh: 政府被指控囤积黄金。

sentences.en: What went into the coffers was real money; what came out was worthless paper.
sentences.zh: 进入金库的都是真金白银；结果出来的是毫无价值的纸。

sentences.en: Bank-Notes, Paper-Money, James Gillray, 1 March 1797.
sentences.zh: 《钞票、纸币》，詹姆斯·吉尔雷 (James Gillray)，1797 年 3 月 1 日。

blocks.html_en: It did not take long for critics, led by Paine and later Cobbett, to read into these apparent ‘safeguards’ sinister designs by bankers and city politicians. Free of the obligation to redeem in gold debts contracted in paper, the Bank of England could in theory print as much paper money as it liked. This enabled the government to run up huge debts to finance the war against the French. While printing more money might sound like a good thing, its effect was inflation, increasing the cost of living during a period of scarcity, poor harvests and rising unemployment. As a popular journalist, Cobbett was one of the first to link the poverty of the people to the ‘money-mongering’ juggle. Week after week in his Political Register, Cobbett attacked the system of paper money and public finance. The articles were collected and published as a book in 1815, Paper against Gold. By July 1817, he claimed to have sold 150,000 copies.

sentences.en: It did not take long for critics, led by Paine and later Cobbett, to read into these apparent ‘safeguards’ sinister designs by bankers and city politicians.
sentences.zh: 没过多久，以潘恩和后来的科贝特为首的批评者就开始解读银行家和城市政客这些明显的“保障措施”的险恶设计。

sentences.en: Free of the obligation to redeem in gold debts contracted in paper, the Bank of England could in theory print as much paper money as it liked.
sentences.zh: 由于没有赎回纸质黄金债务的义务，理论上英格兰银行可以随意印制纸币。

sentences.en: This enabled the government to run up huge debts to finance the war against the French.
sentences.zh: 这使得政府积累了巨额债务来资助对法国的战争。

sentences.en: While printing more money might sound like a good thing, its effect was inflation, increasing the cost of living during a period of scarcity, poor harvests and rising unemployment.
sentences.zh: 虽然印更多的钱听起来像是一件好事，但它的影响是通货膨胀，在物质匮乏、收成不佳和失业率上升的时期增加了生活成本。

sentences.en: As a popular journalist, Cobbett was one of the first to link the poverty of the people to the ‘money-mongering’ juggle.
sentences.zh: 作为一名受欢迎的记者，科贝特是最早将人民的贫困与“金钱交易”联系起来的人之一。

sentences.en: Week after week in his Political Register, Cobbett attacked the system of paper money and public finance.
sentences.zh: 科贝特在他的政治记录中一周又一周地攻击纸币和公共财政体系。

sentences.en: The articles were collected and published as a book in 1815, Paper against Gold.
sentences.zh: 这些文章被收集并于 1815 年出版成书《纸对金》。

sentences.en: By July 1817, he claimed to have sold 150,000 copies.
sentences.zh: 到 1817 年 7 月，他声称已售出 15 万册。

blocks.html_en: The people were poor, Cobbett reasoned, because they had to pay crippling levels of indirect taxation on everyday items to pay down the exorbitant interest on the debt. Worse still, the many wars waged by the British in the 18th century had created a class of idle creditors who were able to enrich themselves by charging high levels of interest which the government was forced to to agree to finance its costly wars.

sentences.en: The people were poor, Cobbett reasoned, because they had to pay crippling levels of indirect taxation on everyday items to pay down the exorbitant interest on the debt.
sentences.zh: 科贝特推断，人们之所以贫穷，是因为他们必须为日常用品缴纳高额的间接税，以偿还过高的债务利息。

sentences.en: Worse still, the many wars waged by the British in the 18th century had created a class of idle creditors who were able to enrich themselves by charging high levels of interest which the government was forced to to agree to finance its costly wars.
sentences.zh: 更糟糕的是，英国在 18 世纪发动的许多战争造就了一个闲散债权人阶层，他们能够通过收取高额利息来中饱私囊，政府被迫同意为昂贵的战争提供资金。

blocks.html_en: But the real scandal in Cobbett’s view was that these debts, contracted in paper money and thus hugely inflated in value, would have to be settled at some point in specie. In other words, theoretical debt would have to be paid off in real money, thus further inflating the value of the debt. It was deeply unfair, so the reasoning went, that a debt contracted in paper at, say the value of £10,000, would now be worth £100,000 because of the higher value of gold. Whose pockets would this hit? The taxpayer.

sentences.en: But the real scandal in Cobbett’s view was that these debts, contracted in paper money and thus hugely inflated in value, would have to be settled at some point in specie.
sentences.zh: 但科贝特认为，真正的丑闻在于，这些债务以纸币形式签订，因而价值大幅膨胀，必须在某个时候以货币来结算。

sentences.en: In other words, theoretical debt would have to be paid off in real money, thus further inflating the value of the debt.
sentences.zh: 换句话说，理论上的债务必须用实际货币偿还，从而进一步抬高债务的价值。

sentences.en: It was deeply unfair, so the reasoning went, that a debt contracted in paper at, say the value of £10,000, would now be worth £100,000 because of the higher value of gold.
sentences.zh: 这是非常不公平的，因此，由于黄金的价值更高，以纸面形式签订的债务（例如价值 10,000 英镑）现在将价值 100,000 英镑。

sentences.en: Whose pockets would this hit?
sentences.zh: 这会砸到谁的口袋呢？

sentences.en: The taxpayer.
sentences.zh: 纳税人。

blocks.html_en: Why was the government determined to resume cash payments given the potential damage it was likely to inflict? Virtually all the experts agreed that convertibility would have to be restored to bring back sound finance and stability. This was the view taken by Lord Liverpool’s government in the 1810s, with the future Tory PM Sir Robert Peel taking charge of the legislation to resume cash payments. But how was this to be achieved without plunging the nation into financial meltdown? The amount of money in circulation would have to be reduced to bring it in line with the amount of gold held in reserve by the Bank of England. In Cobbett’s view what made this all the more unfair was that the necessary ‘belt tightening’ would fall disproportionately on the poor. Those on fixed incomes, dividends and interest payments – i.e. the creditors who had lent the money – would see no drop in the value of their investment. But ordinary people would see a fall in their wages and rising prices.

sentences.en: Why was the government determined to resume cash payments given the potential damage it was likely to inflict?
sentences.zh: 鉴于现金支付可能造成的潜在损害，为什么政府决定恢复现金支付？

sentences.en: Virtually all the experts agreed that convertibility would have to be restored to bring back sound finance and stability.
sentences.zh: 几乎所有专家都同意，必须恢复可兑换性，才能恢复稳健的金融和稳定。

sentences.en: This was the view taken by Lord Liverpool’s government in the 1810s, with the future Tory PM Sir Robert Peel taking charge of the legislation to resume cash payments.
sentences.zh: 这是 1810 年代利物浦勋爵政府所持的观点，后来的保守党首相罗伯特·皮尔爵士负责恢复现金支付的立法。

sentences.en: But how was this to be achieved without plunging the nation into financial meltdown?
sentences.zh: 但如何在不让国家陷入金融崩溃的情况下实现这一目标呢？

sentences.en: The amount of money in circulation would have to be reduced to bring it in line with the amount of gold held in reserve by the Bank of England.
sentences.zh: 流通中的货币数量必须减少，以使其与英格兰银行储备的黄金数量保持一致。

sentences.en: In Cobbett’s view what made this all the more unfair was that the necessary ‘belt tightening’ would fall disproportionately on the poor.

sentences.en: Those on fixed incomes, dividends and interest payments – i.e. the creditors who had lent the money – would see no drop in the value of their investment.

sentences.en: But ordinary people would see a fall in their wages and rising prices.

blocks.html_en: For Cobbett there was only one solution: the partial repudiation of the debt through a scaling down of its value to bring it in line with the deflated economy. This demand was regularly echoed by radicals up and down the country. Cobbett taunted the government of Lord Liverpool saying that it simply would not be able to restore convertibility without scaling down the debt. So confident was Cobbett of this that if the government achieved this he would consent to be ‘broiled alive’ on a gridiron. The gridiron became a ready-made symbol that he and his many followers adopted.

blocks.html_en: As it happens, the government did manage the feat but only, Cobbett was quick to allege, by cheating. The government empowered the Bank of England to print large quantities of small value paper notes. This was to ward off financial collapse due to money becoming scarcer as its value increased. Cobbett was proven wrong in that cash payments were eventually resumed (in May 1821), but he was correct that there was nowhere near enough gold in reserve for the Bank to redeem all of its notes in specie, just as there is not today. Cobbett refused to believe that confidence was sufficient to keep a nation’s finances afloat.

blocks.html_en: To make matters worse, this belt-tightening occurred during a period when the nation’s coinage was in serious disarray and short supply. Debasing, counterfeiting, the semi-legal use of tokens and even the use of foreign coins were all widely practised in the 1810s as a strategy of simply making do. This was the context for the dramatic rise in forgery. While forgery was not exclusive to London – Birmingham seems to have been another centre – the problem was much worse in the capital than elsewhere. This was partly due to London’s size, the sheer amount of money circulating there and the ready supply of materials and skills. But it was also because it was much easier and more profitable to forge notes in London than in the provinces. In the latter, country banks, which were independent of the Bank of England, issued their own notes and circulation was largely limited to a local and regional orbit. In this close-knit environment it was more difficult to utter forged bank notes as people were more suspicious of strangers bearing bank notes.

blocks.html_en: In London forgers faced fewer obstacles. The Bank of England seemed powerless, even though each year saw more convictions for forgery. By 1817 more than one in twenty £2 or £1 Bank of England notes were forged – and these were just the ones the Bank knew about. In 1817 the total number of forged £2 and £1 notes returned to the Bank of England was 28,648; in 2016 a mere 2,000 counterfeit £5 notes were removed from circulation. While the total number of forged notes is much higher today than it was 200 years ago, with the vast majority being counterfeit £20 notes, as a proportion of the total number of bank notes in circulation the percentage is much lower.

blocks.html_en: Building on the platform he had constructed in Paper against Gold, Cobbett seized on the forgery cases as further evidence of the iniquities of the paper money system. Since paper money was fiction, Cobbett reasoned that there was, in fact, no difference between bank notes and forged notes; one was legal forgery, the other illegal. Capitalism and counterfeiting were different sides of the same coin: the transition to a paper-based currency and the attendant proliferation of notes, often of poor quality, created ideal conditions for counterfeiting. After all, the currency of both rested on confidence and, while forged notes might be bad money, legal notes might be issued by a bad bank (i.e. one unable to meet its obligations). The British government had even entered into the business of counterfeiting in the 1790s when it had the currency of the French revolutionaries, the assignats, forged in London and circulated in France to try and undermine the new regime.

blocks.html_en: With Cobbett in exile in the United States, having fled to avoid imprisonment for spreading sedition, it fell to T.J. Wooler to lead the public outcry. Through the pages of the Black Dwarf Wooler stoked anger against the forgery convictions, marshalling it into a determined campaign to abolish capital punishment for all crimes except murder. The necessity of such reform became very apparent during the forgery trials between September and December 1818. The trials revealed that it was difficult to distinguish between a forged and a genuine bank note, largely because of the poor quality of the latter. The arbitrary manner in which bank officials determined which notes were forged, and their refusal to divulge the criteria against which they made such determinations, outraged Wooler and the public.

blocks.html_en: Just under a third of those hanged in London in the period 1800-18 were forgers/utterers, some of them poor women driven to the crime as a means of survival. Some of the letters written to the Bank from those convicted for forgery requesting assistance attribute their deviation from the path of rectitude to the prevailing distress in the postwar years. This made the Bank’s prosecutions look like petty acts of class war. The Bank’s ruthlessness in pursuing forgers represented in microcosm the wider fears of the commercial classes. Where Cobbett and Wooler led in print, George Cruikshank and William Hone followed in visual satire by producing the most iconic rendition of the forgery crisis with his spoof counterfeit Bank Restriction Note and Barometer (1819).

sentences.en: Just under a third of those hanged in London in the period 1800-18 were forgers/utterers, some of them poor women driven to the crime as a means of survival.
sentences.zh: 1800-18 年期间，在伦敦被绞死的人中，有不到三分之一是伪造者/口述者，其中一些是为了生存而被迫犯罪的贫穷妇女。

sentences.en: Some of the letters written to the Bank from those convicted for forgery requesting assistance attribute their deviation from the path of rectitude to the prevailing distress in the postwar years.
sentences.zh: 那些因伪造文件而被定罪的人写给世界银行的一些请求援助的信件将他们偏离正直道路的原因归因于战后普遍存在的困境。

sentences.en: This made the Bank’s prosecutions look like petty acts of class war.
sentences.zh: 这使得银行的起诉看起来像是阶级战争的小行为。

sentences.en: The Bank’s ruthlessness in pursuing forgers represented in microcosm the wider fears of the commercial classes.
sentences.zh: 央行对造假者的残酷追捕是商业阶层更广泛恐惧的缩影。

sentences.en: Where Cobbett and Wooler led in print, George Cruikshank and William Hone followed in visual satire by producing the most iconic rendition of the forgery crisis with his spoof counterfeit Bank Restriction Note and Barometer (1819).
sentences.zh: 科贝特和伍勒在印刷品方面处于领先地位，乔治·克鲁克尚克和威廉·霍恩在视觉讽刺方面紧随其后，通过恶搞的伪造《银行限制票据和晴雨表》（1819）对伪造危机进行了最具标志性的演绎。

blocks.html_en: The crime of counterfeiting was eventually declassified as a capital offence in 1836. Thereafter conviction rates for forgery shot up. Now that the crime was no longer a capital offence juries were more likely to return guilty verdicts. Certainly, judging by the number of trials at the Old Bailey, there had been no decline in ‘coining’ offences. The high number of offences in 1817-21 (with a peak of 78 offences in 1818), following a lull in the 1820s, were easily surpassed in the mid-1830s. There were 90 offences in 1835 alone. Yet conviction rates were on average much higher in the 1830s and 1840s than they had been in the 1810s and 20s: in 1818, 78 per cent of those on trial had been found guilty; by 1836 the figure was 99 per cent. The Bank of England had begun to turn the tables in its favour.

sentences.en: The crime of counterfeiting was eventually declassified as a capital offence in 1836.
sentences.zh: 1836 年，伪造罪最终被解除为死刑罪。

sentences.en: Thereafter conviction rates for forgery shot up.
sentences.zh: 此后，伪造罪的定罪率猛增。

sentences.en: Now that the crime was no longer a capital offence juries were more likely to return guilty verdicts.
sentences.zh: 既然该罪行不再是死刑，陪审团更有可能做出有罪判决。

sentences.en: Certainly, judging by the number of trials at the Old Bailey, there had been no decline in ‘coining’ offences.
sentences.zh: 当然，从老贝利法院的审判数量来看，“造币”犯罪并没有减少。

sentences.en: The high number of offences in 1817-21 (with a peak of 78 offences in 1818), following a lull in the 1820s, were easily surpassed in the mid-1830s.
sentences.zh: 继 1820 年代的平静之后，1817-21 年的犯罪数量很高（1818 年达到高峰，有 78 起犯罪），在 1830 年代中期很容易被超越。

sentences.en: There were 90 offences in 1835 alone.
sentences.zh: 仅 1835 年就有 90 起违法行为。

sentences.en: Yet conviction rates were on average much higher in the 1830s and 1840s than they had been in the 1810s and 20s: in 1818, 78 per cent of those on trial had been found guilty; by 1836 the figure was 99 per cent.
sentences.zh: 然而，1830 年代和 1840 年代的平均定罪率比 1810 年代和 20 年代高得多：1818 年，78% 的受审者被判有罪；1818 年，78% 的受审者被判有罪；到 1836 年，这一数字达到了 99%。

sentences.en: The Bank of England had begun to turn the tables in its favour.
sentences.zh: 英格兰银行已经开始扭转局势，对自己有利。

blocks.html_en: Two hundred years ago the Bank of England was forced to confront some of the same questions it faces today: What is money? What should it be made of? What does it represent? And what should be done regarding the attendant problems of counterfeiting? One consequence of the forgery trials was the introduction of banknotes that were more difficult to counterfeit. The new £5 and £10 notes are merely the latest in a long line of anti-counterfeiting measures. Neither are a crippling National Debt or accusations of unholy alliances between city bankers and politicians new. Cobbett would have found today’s City much as he left it in 1835.

sentences.en: Two hundred years ago the Bank of England was forced to confront some of the same questions it faces today: What is money?
sentences.zh: 200 年前，英格兰银行被迫面对今天所面临的一些相同问题：什么是货币？

sentences.en: What should it be made of?
sentences.zh: 它应该由什么制成？

sentences.en: What does it represent?
sentences.zh: 它代表什么？

sentences.en: And what should be done regarding the attendant problems of counterfeiting?
sentences.zh: 对于随之而来的假冒问题应该采取什么措施？

sentences.en: One consequence of the forgery trials was the introduction of banknotes that were more difficult to counterfeit.
sentences.zh: 伪造试验的结果之一是推出了更难伪造的纸币。

sentences.en: The new £5 and £10 notes are merely the latest in a long line of anti-counterfeiting measures.
sentences.zh: 新的 5 英镑和 10 英镑纸币只是一系列防伪措施中的最新举措。

sentences.en: Neither are a crippling National Debt or accusations of unholy alliances between city bankers and politicians new.
sentences.zh: 严重的国家债务或城市银行家与政客之间邪恶联盟的指控都不是新鲜事。

sentences.en: Cobbett would have found today’s City much as he left it in 1835.
sentences.zh: 科贝特会发现今天的这座城市与他 1835 年离开时的样子没什么不同。

blocks.html_en: Matthew Roberts is Senior Lecturer in Modern British History at Sheffield Hallam University.

sentences.en: Matthew Roberts is Senior Lecturer in Modern British History at Sheffield Hallam University.
sentences.zh: 马修·罗伯茨是谢菲尔德哈勒姆大学英国现代史高级讲师。

content_html_zh:
19 世纪初，“肮脏的破布”（或纸币）成为一种常见的货币形式。 随后，伪造案件激增，严厉起诉也随之激增。 我们是如何从金子变成纸的？

英格兰银行的景观，由托马斯·霍斯默·谢泼德 (Thomas Hosmer Shepherd) 绘制，1816 年。 国立博物馆。 公共领域。

1820年，伦敦街头出现了一本名为《撒旦的钞票》的讽刺小册子。 伴随着五名男子与坐在绞刑架上的魔鬼一起被处决的木刻，这本小册子对拿破仑战争结束后几年在英国爆发的伪造审判的流行进行了尖锐的评论。 这位匿名作者将责任完全归咎于英格兰银行及其最大的债务人——当时的政府：

在伦敦的“Change”附近有一座房子，（我不愿意说出它的名字）那里出售碎布，每英镑约翰牛给二十先令。

作者的匿名以及他们不愿明确提及英格兰银行的名字，无疑是因为担心因诽谤或煽动叛乱而被起诉。 自从 1797 年威廉·皮特政府实际上暂停了金本位制以来，“可怜的”约翰·布尔就被迫接受纸币来代替黄金和白银。 这是第一次，但绝不是最后一次，这个国家开始思考：金钱到底是什么？

最近推出的新纸币和硬币又再次引发了这个问题。 它是由什么制成的重要吗？ 金钱什么时候才成为一种方便的交换媒介而不是代表内在价值？ 或者，换句话说，我们是如何从黄金变成纸的？ 新版 5 英镑和 10 英镑纸币被一些人斥为大富翁或塑料货币，尤其受到严厉批评。 英格兰银行声称，聚合物材料的选择以及许多其他安全功能使新纸币比以往更难伪造。 当然，英格兰银行的目标是领先伪造者一步。

200 年前的 1817 年，英格兰银行根本就比伪造者领先一步。 在有关货币性质和用途的类似争论中，流通中的伪造纸币和硬币数量创下历史新高。 在老贝利进行了一系列备受瞩目的审判后，该银行决定惩罚那些被判犯有伪造罪的人，这引起了公众的强烈抗议。 被判犯有伪造罪并被判处死刑的人数空前。 从技术上讲，造假罪是一种叛国罪，是一种重罪。 绝大多数被定罪的人都是穷人，其中很多是妇女。 他们的罪行通常不是制造伪造的纸币，而是“说出”：持有或投入流通伪造的纸币。 激进的记者 T.J. 1818 年 10 月，争论最激烈时，伍勒发出了这样的感叹。

当时对政府货币政策的主要批评者是威廉·科贝特（William Cobbett，1763-1835 年），他是一名从士兵保守派转变为激进的记者。 对于科贝特和成千上万支持他的贫困劳动人民来说，纸币根本不是钱。 在他们看来，只有硬币——主要是黄金——才是真正的货币。 与纸张不同，贵金属具有内在价值。 这是带来稳定的“金本位”的支柱之一。 金本位制的基本原则是流通中的纸币数量必须以英格兰银行持有的固定数量的黄金作为支持。 科贝特将这些钞票称为“肮脏的破布”，只不过是纸质承诺。 正如激进的托马斯·潘恩在 1790 年代指出的那样，纯纸币体系完全依赖于信心，而信心可能会瞬间消失。 潘恩将纸币描述为“沉睡的怀疑”并非没有道理。

潘恩和科贝特借鉴了一系列的货币理论，认为货币必须以贵金属为基础。 这并不排除使用纸币，只要它们可以兑换成硬币。 正是这种保证给了货币以及金融体系的稳定性。 至今仍出现在纸币上的保证书承诺按要求向持票人支付所需金额的现金，但 1797 年之后却是一个谎言。 如今，我们很少有人停下来思考这句话的真正含义。 如今，它只不过是一种象征性的生存。但在 18 世纪，这意味着可以将纸币带到银行兑换成等值的硬币。 换句话说，它们就像支票。 这种情况在拿破仑战争期间发生了变化，当时皮特政府在人们对法国入侵的担忧日益加剧之际，为了阻止英镑挤兑，于 1797 年暂停了英镑兑换。

这种现金支付的暂停一直持续到 1821 年。 西班牙与法国联手对抗英国及其盟友后，可怜的约翰·布尔被迫靠纸币和从西班牙船只上缴获的西班牙元重新发行来维持生计。 政府被指控囤积黄金。 进入金库的都是真金白银；结果出来的是毫无价值的纸。

没过多久，以潘恩和后来的科贝特为首的批评者就开始解读银行家和城市政客这些明显的“保障措施”的险恶设计。 由于没有赎回纸质黄金债务的义务，理论上英格兰银行可以随意印制纸币。 这使得政府积累了巨额债务来资助对法国的战争。 虽然印更多的钱听起来像是一件好事，但它的影响是通货膨胀，在物质匮乏、收成不佳和失业率上升的时期增加了生活成本。 作为一名受欢迎的记者，科贝特是最早将人民的贫困与“金钱交易”联系起来的人之一。 科贝特在他的政治记录中一周又一周地攻击纸币和公共财政体系。 这些文章被收集并于 1815 年出版成书《纸对金》。 到 1817 年 7 月，他声称已售出 15 万册。

科贝特推断，人们之所以贫穷，是因为他们必须为日常用品缴纳高额的间接税，以偿还过高的债务利息。 更糟糕的是，英国在 18 世纪发动的许多战争造就了一个闲散债权人阶层，他们能够通过收取高额利息来中饱私囊，政府被迫同意为昂贵的战争提供资金。

但科贝特认为，真正的丑闻在于，这些债务以纸币形式签订，因而价值大幅膨胀，必须在某个时候以货币来结算。 换句话说，理论上的债务必须用实际货币偿还，从而进一步抬高债务的价值。 这是非常不公平的，因此，由于黄金的价值更高，以纸面形式签订的债务（例如价值 10,000 英镑）现在将价值 100,000 英镑。 这会砸到谁的口袋呢？ 纳税人。

鉴于现金支付可能造成的潜在损害，为什么政府决定恢复现金支付？ 几乎所有专家都同意，必须恢复可兑换性，才能恢复稳健的金融和稳定。 这是 1810 年代利物浦勋爵政府所持的观点，后来的保守党首相罗伯特·皮尔爵士负责恢复现金支付的立法。 但如何在不让国家陷入金融崩溃的情况下实现这一目标呢？ 流通中的货币数量必须减少，以使其与英格兰银行储备的黄金数量保持一致。 在科贝特看来，让这一切变得更加不公平的是，必要的“勒紧裤腰带”将不成比例地落在穷人身上。 那些靠固定收益、股息和利息支付的人——即借钱的债权人——他们的投资价值不会下降。 但普通老百姓的工资会下降，物价会上涨。

对于科贝特来说，只有一个解决方案：通过缩小债务价值来部分拒绝债务，使其与通货紧缩的经济保持一致。 这一要求经常得到全国各地激进分子的响应。 科贝特嘲笑利物浦勋爵政府，称如果不减少债务规模，它根本无法恢复可兑换性。 科贝特对此非常有信心，如果政府实现了这一目标，他就会同意在烤架上“活活烤”。 烤架成为他和他的许多追随者所采用的现成符号。

碰巧的是，政府确实实现了这一壮举，但科贝特很快声称，只是通过作弊。 政府授权英格兰银行印刷大量小额纸币。 这是为了避免由于货币随着价值的增加而变得越来越稀缺而导致的金融崩溃。 事实证明，科贝特的错误在于最终恢复了现金支付（1821 年 5 月），但他的观点是正确的，即银行储备的黄金远远不足以以实物形式赎回所有纸币，就像今天一样。 科贝特拒绝相信信心足以维持一个国家的财政状况。

更糟糕的是，这种紧缩开支发生在国家铸币严重混乱和供应短缺的时期。 贬低、伪造、半合法地使用代币，甚至使用外国硬币，都在 1810 年代被广泛采用，作为一种简单的凑合策略。 这就是伪造品急剧增加的背景。 虽然伪造并非伦敦独有——伯明翰似乎是另一个中心——但首都的问题比其他地方严重得多。 这在一定程度上是由于伦敦的规模、那里流通的资金量巨大以及材料和技能的现成供应。 但这也是因为在伦敦铸造纸币比在外省更容易、更有利可图。 在后者中，独立于英格兰银行的乡村银行发行自己的纸币，流通范围主要限于当地和区域范围。 在这种紧密联系的环境中，伪造钞票变得更加困难，因为人们对携带钞票的陌生人更加怀疑。

在伦敦，造假者面临的障碍较少。 尽管每年都有更多的伪造罪被定罪，但英格兰银行似乎无能为力。 到 1817 年，超过二十分之一的 2 英镑或 1 英镑英格兰银行纸币是伪造的——而这些只是该银行所知道的。 1817 年，退回英格兰银行的伪造 2 英镑和 1 英镑纸币总数为 28,648 张； 2016 年，仅有 2,000 张 5 英镑假钞被从流通中下架。 虽然今天的伪造纸币总数比 200 年前高得多，其中绝大多数是伪造的 20 英镑纸币，但占流通纸币总数的比例却要低得多。

人们宣讲伪造文件的罪恶，并详细讲述了死刑判决通过后被判有罪的人所遭受的心理恐惧的可怕细节。 但没有什么，甚至死亡的威胁，都不足以阻止造假者。 到 1817 年 12 月，情况变得如此严峻，以至于英格兰银行恢复了已解散的委员会，以审查改进纸币和防止伪造的计划。 建议包括使用隐形墨水、由艺术家绘制的令人难以置信的华丽设计、独特且优质的纸张和水印，以及需要最先进的印刷技术来超越伪造者。 该委员会报告称

一些人抱有很大的期望，认为近年来取得如此奇妙发现的化学科学可能是改进纸张材料或墨水成分的手段，或者是通过提供区分真假钞票的测试的手段。

最终，该银行采用了这些功能的组合，并将继续这样做。 正如委员会承认的那样，银行所能期望的最好结果就是领先伪造者一步：

可以肯定的是，王国硬币的伪造者和钞票的伪造者都联合起来...... 通常，同一个人都是具有伟大创造力和多才多艺的人，因此可以推测他们的努力将与可能采取的任何措施保持同步并不过分。

与此同时，英国央行建立了一个令人印象深刻的侦探和线人网络，建立了英国现代历史上组织最完善的自诉机构之一。 在富而德律师事务所的策划下，该银行的董事们慷慨地动用了大量资金来起诉伪造者。 不遗余力。 对导致定罪的信息提供奖励。 英格兰银行的档案中保存着供词、辩诉交易以及有关伪造的各种信息。 最完整的叙述之一是由雷丁监狱的囚犯爱德华·埃维尔 (Edward Ewer) 于 1820 年提供的。 市场和展会似乎是假货的运作中心：

几乎没有摊主、鱼贩或马贩经常光顾所有的集市，但交易的是伪造的纸币和基础硬币。 没有它他们就无法生存。 他们出售的商品价格低于购买的价格，并且只保留摊位作为盲人。

科贝特以他在《纸币对抗黄金》中构建的平台为基础，抓住了伪造案件作为纸币体系不公正的进一步证据。 由于纸币是虚构的，科贝特推断纸币和伪造的纸币实际上没有区别；一种是合法的伪造，另一种是非法的。 资本主义和造假是同一枚硬币的不同侧面：向纸质货币的过渡以及随之而来的纸币的激增（通常质量较差）为造假创造了理想的条件。 毕竟，两者的货币都依赖于信心，虽然伪造的纸币可能是劣币，但合法纸币可能是由坏银行（即无法履行其义务的银行）发行的。 英国政府甚至在 1790 年代涉足造假业务，当时英国政府拥有法国革命者的货币“指币”，在伦敦铸造并在法国流通，试图破坏新政权。

科贝特因煽动叛乱而流亡美国，以避免入狱，此事落到了 T.J. 伍勒引发了公众的强烈抗议。 通过《黑矮人伍勒》的书页激起了人们对伪造定罪的愤怒，并将其整合为一场坚决的运动，废除对除谋杀之外的所有罪行的死刑。 在 1818 年 9 月至 12 月的伪造审判中，这种改革的必要性变得非常明显。 试验表明，很难区分伪造的钞票和真钞，很大程度上是因为后者的质量较差。 银行官员任意判定哪些钞票是伪造的，并且拒绝透露做出此类判定所依据的标准，这激怒了伍勒和公众。

1800-18 年期间，在伦敦被绞死的人中，有不到三分之一是伪造者/口述者，其中一些是为了生存而被迫犯罪的贫穷妇女。 那些因伪造文件而被定罪的人写给世界银行的一些请求援助的信件将他们偏离正直道路的原因归因于战后普遍存在的困境。 这使得银行的起诉看起来像是阶级战争的小行为。 央行对造假者的残酷追捕是商业阶层更广泛恐惧的缩影。 科贝特和伍勒在印刷品方面处于领先地位，乔治·克鲁克尚克和威廉·霍恩在视觉讽刺方面紧随其后，通过恶搞的伪造《银行限制票据和晴雨表》（1819）对伪造危机进行了最具标志性的演绎。

1836 年，伪造罪最终被解除为死刑罪。 此后，伪造罪的定罪率猛增。 既然该罪行不再是死刑，陪审团更有可能做出有罪判决。 当然，从老贝利法院的审判数量来看，“造币”犯罪并没有减少。 继 1820 年代的平静之后，1817-21 年的犯罪数量很高（1818 年达到高峰，有 78 起犯罪），在 1830 年代中期很容易被超越。 仅 1835 年就有 90 起违法行为。 然而，1830 年代和 1840 年代的平均定罪率比 1810 年代和 20 年代高得多：1818 年，78% 的受审者被判有罪；1818 年，78% 的受审者被判有罪；到 1836 年，这一数字达到了 99%。 英格兰银行已经开始扭转局势，对自己有利。

200 年前，英格兰银行被迫面对今天所面临的一些相同问题：什么是货币？ 它应该由什么制成？ 它代表什么？ 对于随之而来的假冒问题应该采取什么措施？ 伪造试验的结果之一是推出了更难伪造的纸币。 新的 5 英镑和 10 英镑纸币只是一系列防伪措施中的最新举措。 严重的国家债务或城市银行家与政客之间邪恶联盟的指控都不是新鲜事。 科贝特会发现今天的这座城市与他 1835 年离开时的样子没什么不同。

马修·罗伯茨是谢菲尔德哈勒姆大学英国现代史高级讲师。
